title: Genesis AI is in talks to raise about $500m for its robot brains
excerpt: Genesis AI, the startup building foundation models that it hopes will run inside a wide range of robots, is in talks to raise about $500m, according to Bloomberg. A round of that size would value the company at roughly $3bn, capping a fast climb for a business that only came out of stealth last summer. […]
content_html:
Genesis AI, the startup building foundation models that it hopes will run inside a wide range of robots, is in talks to raise about $500m, according to Bloomberg.

A round of that size would value the company at roughly $3bn, capping a fast climb for a business that only came out of stealth last summer.

Bloomberg reported the discussions on July 23, citing people familiar with the matter. The figures come with the usual caveats attached to private fundraising, where rounds move, shrink, and occasionally fall apart before anyone signs.

The startup, which last month unveiled a wheeled robot called Eno as its answer to humanoid hype, had not confirmed the raise at the time of writing.

Reporting that followed put more shape on the deal. Premji Invest is said to be in discussions to lead the round, with HSG, formerly Sequoia China, and Northzone weighing further investment.

The 💜 of EU tech

The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now!

The $3bn figure is described as a pre-money valuation. None of the firms has commented, and the line-up could still shift.

What Genesis is selling is software rather than steel. Its universal robotics model, GENE-26.5, is meant to act as a shared brain across different machines, trained in part on synthetic data from an in-house physics engine the company says runs scenarios hundreds of thousands of times faster than real time.

The pitch has landed at a moment when investors are pouring money into so-called physical AI, the same appetite that recently lifted NEURA Robotics to a round of up to $1.4bn.

Genesis has positioned itself against rivals such as Skild AI and Physical Intelligence partly on openness, saying it intends to open-source parts of its simulation stack.

The approach is deliberately full-stack. Genesis designs both the model and the dexterous, human-like hands it runs on, aiming to close what it calls the “embodiment gap” between data gathered from human movement and the machines meant to reproduce it.

A roughly $300 sensor glove, worn by workers to capture training data, sits at the centre of that plan.

Should it close, the raise would slot Genesis into a run of outsized robotics cheques. London’s Humanoid banked a $152m Series A, and Standard Bots reached a $1bn valuation on a $200m round.

The wager across all of them is that general-purpose robots are the next frontier after chatbots, and backers are pricing the bet accordingly.

Genesis emerged from stealth in July 2025 with a $105m seed round co-led by Eclipse Ventures and Khosla Ventures, among the larger seeds of that year.

Backers included former Google chief executive Eric Schmidt, French billionaire Xavier Niel, Bpifrance, and HSG, the same firm now said to be circling the new round.

A $500m follow-on inside 12 months would mark a steep markup. The exact structure of the new round, including how much is primary capital and when it might close, has not been disclosed.

The company was co-founded by Zhou Xian, a Carnegie Mellon robotics PhD who serves as chief executive, and Théophile Gervet, a former research scientist at Mistral AI.

It keeps offices in Paris and the San Francisco Bay Area, with a more recent push into London, and employs around 60 people split roughly evenly between Europe and the US.

The startup should not be confused with the open-source Genesis physics-simulation project or the various other firms trading under the same name, a recurring hazard in a crowded robotics field.

Genesis frames its target as the global market for physical labour, which it has valued at roughly $40 trillion, the kind of number that makes a $3bn valuation read as modest and a $500m round as a down payment.

Whether the talks convert into a signed term sheet is a separate matter. For now the figure is a report, not a deal.
content_text: Genesis AI, the startup building foundation models that it hopes will run inside a wide range of robots, is in talks to raise about $500m, according to Bloomberg. A round of that size would value the company at roughly $3bn, capping a fast climb for a business that only came out of stealth last summer. Bloomberg reported the discussions on July 23, citing people familiar with the matter. The figures come with the usual caveats attached to private fundraising, where rounds move, shrink, and occasionally fall apart before anyone signs. The startup, which last month unveiled a wheeled robot called Eno as its answer to humanoid hype, had not confirmed the raise at the time of writing. Reporting that followed put more shape on the deal. Premji Invest is said to be in discussions to lead the round, with HSG, formerly Sequoia China, and Northzone weighing further investment.The 💜 of EU techThe latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now! The $3bn figure is described as a pre-money valuation. None of the firms has commented, and the line-up could still shift. What Genesis is selling is software rather than steel. Its universal robotics model, GENE-26.5, is meant to act as a shared brain across different machines, trained in part on synthetic data from an in-house physics engine the company says runs scenarios hundreds of thousands of times faster than real time. The pitch has landed at a moment when investors are pouring money into so-called physical AI, the same appetite that recently lifted NEURA Robotics to a round of up to $1.4bn. Genesis has positioned itself against rivals such as Skild AI and Physical Intelligence partly on openness, saying it intends to open-source parts of its simulation stack. The approach is deliberately full-stack. Genesis designs both the model and the dexterous, human-like hands it runs on, aiming to close what it calls the “embodiment gap” between data gathered from human movement and the machines meant to reproduce it. A roughly $300 sensor glove, worn by workers to capture training data, sits at the centre of that plan. Should it close, the raise would slot Genesis into a run of outsized robotics cheques. London’s Humanoid banked a $152m Series A, and Standard Bots reached a $1bn valuation on a $200m round. The wager across all of them is that general-purpose robots are the next frontier after chatbots, and backers are pricing the bet accordingly. Genesis emerged from stealth in July 2025 with a $105m seed round co-led by Eclipse Ventures and Khosla Ventures, among the larger seeds of that year. Backers included former Google chief executive Eric Schmidt, French billionaire Xavier Niel, Bpifrance, and HSG, the same firm now said to be circling the new round. A $500m follow-on inside 12 months would mark a steep markup. The exact structure of the new round, including how much is primary capital and when it might close, has not been disclosed. The company was co-founded by Zhou Xian, a Carnegie Mellon robotics PhD who serves as chief executive, and Théophile Gervet, a former research scientist at Mistral AI. It keeps offices in Paris and the San Francisco Bay Area, with a more recent push into London, and employs around 60 people split roughly evenly between Europe and the US. The startup should not be confused with the open-source Genesis physics-simulation project or the various other firms trading under the same name, a recurring hazard in a crowded robotics field. Genesis frames its target as the global market for physical labour, which it has valued at roughly $40 trillion, the kind of number that makes a $3bn valuation read as modest and a $500m round as a down payment. Whether the talks convert into a signed term sheet is a separate matter. For now the figure is a report, not a deal.

sentences.zh: Genesis 将其目标定为全球实体劳动力市场，其估值约为 40 万亿美元，这个数字使得 30 亿美元的估值显得适中，而一轮 5 亿美元的融资则作为首付。

content_html_zh:
据彭博社报道，Genesis AI 是一家构建基础模型的初创公司，希望能够在各种机器人中运行，该公司正在洽谈融资约 5 亿美元。

如此规模的一轮融资将使该公司的估值约为 30 亿美元，这限制了这家去年夏天才崭露头角的企业的快速攀升。

彭博社 7 月 23 日援引知情人士的话报道了此次讨论。 这些数字伴随着私人筹款的常见警告，即融资轮次发生变化、缩减，有时甚至在任何人签字之前就崩溃了。

这家初创公司上个月推出了一款名为 Eno 的轮式机器人，作为对人形炒作的回应，但在撰写本文时尚未确认此次加薪。

随后的报道进一步明确了这笔交易。 据称，Premji Invest 正在与 HSG（前红杉中国）和 Northzone 讨论牵头此轮融资的事宜，并考虑进一步投资。

欧盟科技的💜

来自欧盟科技界的最新传言、我们明智的创始人鲍里斯的故事，以及一些值得怀疑的人工智能艺术。 每周都会免费发送到您的收件箱中。 立即注册！

30 亿美元的数字被描述为投资前估值。 没有一家公司对此发表评论，而且阵容仍可能发生变化。

Genesis 销售的是软件而不是钢铁。 其通用机器人模型 GENE-26.5 旨在充当不同机器之间的共享大脑，部分接受来自内部物理引擎的合成数据的训练，该公司表示，运行场景的速度比实时速度快数十万倍。

此次推介活动正值投资者纷纷向所谓的物理人工智能领域投入大量资金，同样的兴趣最近使 NEURA Robotics 的融资额达到了 14 亿美元。

Genesis 将自己定位于与 Skild AI 和 Physical Intelligence 等竞争对手抗衡，部分原因在于开放性，并表示打算开源部分模拟堆栈。

该方法是有意采用全栈的。 Genesis 设计了模型和它所运行的灵巧的类人手，旨在缩小从人类运动收集的数据与复制这些数据的机器之间所谓的“体现差距”。

该计划的核心是一只价值约 300 美元的传感器手套，供工作人员佩戴以捕获训练数据。

如果该交易结束，加薪将使创世纪陷入一系列巨额机器人支票中。 伦敦的 Humanoid 获得了 1.52 亿美元的 A 轮融资，Standard Bots 在 2 亿美元的融资中达到了 10 亿美元的估值。

他们所有人的赌注是，通用机器人是继聊天机器人之后的下一个前沿领域，支持者也相应地为赌注定价。

Genesis 于 2025 年 7 月从隐秘状态中脱颖而出，获得了由 Eclipse Ventures 和 Khosla Ventures 共同领投的 1.05 亿美元种子轮融资，这是当年规模较大的种子轮融资之一。

支持者包括谷歌前首席执行官埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt）、法国亿万富翁泽维尔·尼尔（Xavier Niel）、Bpifrance 和 HSG（据称该公司现在正在参与新一轮融资）。

12 个月内追加 5 亿美元的后续资金将标志着大幅上涨。 新一轮融资的具体结构，包括主要资本的金额以及何时结束，尚未披露。

该公司由担任首席执行官的卡内基梅隆大学机器人学博士周贤和 Mistral AI 前研究科学家 Théophile Gervet 共同创立。

该公司在巴黎和旧金山湾区设有办事处，最近又进军伦敦，拥有约 60 名员工，分布在欧洲和美国。

这家初创公司不应与开源 Genesis 物理模拟项目或以相同名称进行交易的其他各种公司相混淆，这是拥挤的机器人领域中反复出现的危险。

Genesis 将其目标定为全球实体劳动力市场，其估值约为 40 万亿美元，这个数字使得 30 亿美元的估值显得适中，而一轮 5 亿美元的融资则作为首付。

谈判是否转化为签署的条款清单是另一回事。 目前这个数字只是一份报告，而不是一项交易。
